destinations.evidence: Scheduled block math is centralized in saturating helpers; compute_max_block_number_u64 returns 0 for num_calls == 0 and uses saturating_add/saturating_mul for the remaining terms.
destinations.snippet:
if num_calls == 0 { return 0; }
(start_block as u64)
    .saturating_add((frequency as u64).saturating_mul(num_calls as u64 - 1))
    .saturating_add(ttl as u64)
...
self.expected_block(execution_index).saturating_add(self.ttl as u64)

destinations.evidence: Payload-builder obligation derivation calls the shared due_index_bounds helper and therefore inherits its zero-num_calls and saturating due-window behavior.

equivalence_argument: Mapped in the port: scheduled max-block, expected-block, TTL deadline, and builder due-window calculations use shared saturating u64 helpers and return no execution window for num_calls == 0.
rationale: The later candidates consolidate overflow handling for reconstructed scheduled transactions and ScheduledCall helper calculations.
statement: Scheduled expected_block, ttl_deadline, expiry_block, and payload-builder max_block_number derivation must use saturating arithmetic, with expiry_block returning 0 for num_calls == 0, so malformed or extreme schedule fields cannot panic or wrap during augmentation or verification.
suggested_falsification_tests: Construct ScheduledCall values with num_calls=0, u32::MAX frequency, u32::MAX start_block, and u32::MAX ttl; assert expiry_block returns 0 for zero calls and saturates rather than panicking or wrapping for extreme values.
Run compute_obligations_from_calls on an extreme call snapshot and assert due_index_bounds either returns a valid bounded interval or None without overflow.
summary: Scheduled block math is overflow-safe in the port.
test_or_falsification_candidate: Add overflow-boundary unit tests for compute_max_block_number_u64, ScheduledCall::ttl_deadline, expiry_block, and compute_obligations_from_calls.
title: Scheduled block math is overflow safe
